title: The Lobito Corridor shows how digitization is shaping critical minerals competition
gist: Two July 2026 investment deals have re-centered attention on the Lobito Corridor railway linking Angola, the DRC and Zambia, but Chatham House argues that digitization of mineral supply chains - geological databases, cadastres and traceability platforms - is the under-appreciated factor that will decide whether the Copperbelt is genuinely economically transformed, and it currently lags physical infrastructure while China builds a rival fibre network.

insights.claim: On 3 July 2026 the US International Development Finance Corporation and the Development Bank of Southern Africa secured a $753-million package to rehabilitate and upgrade the 1,300-km single-track Lobito Atlantic Railway from Lobito to the DRC border.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Signals concrete Western capital deployment into the Copperbelt corridor - watch for follow-on offtake and mining project financing tied to the rail link.
insights.quote: the US International Development Finance Corporation and the Development Bank of Southern Africa secured a $753-million package to rehabilitate and upgrade the 1,300-km single-track railway from Lobito to the DRC border
insights.locator: On 3 July, the US International Development

insights.claim: On 10 July 2026 the DRC government approved a partnership with Portuguese construction company Mota-Engil for a complete rehabilitation of the railway line from Angola's border to the copper and cobalt producing regions in the DRC.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Mota-Engil gains exposure to a strategically backed corridor; construction and logistics players positioned on Lobito stand to benefit from the rehabilitation pipeline.
insights.quote: the DRC government approved a partnership with Portuguese construction company Mota-Engil for a complete rehabilitation of the railway line from Angola’s border to the copper and cobalt producing regions in the DRC
insights.locator: On 10 July, the DRC government approved

insights.claim: The Lobito Corridor railway is a Western-backed initiative embedded in geopolitical competition between the US and its European partners and China, the dominant international presence in the region, over infrastructure, mineral production and development opportunities in Africa's strategic Copperbelt.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Frames Lobito as a proxy for US-China resource competition - critical-minerals investors should treat corridor developments as geopolitically contingent, not purely commercial.
insights.quote: part of wider geopolitical competition between the US (and its European partners) and China – the dominant international presence in the region
insights.locator: These moves are part of wider geopolitical

insights.claim: For the cross-border mining ecosystem centred on the Lobito Corridor to achieve genuine economic transformation, it must be digitized, yet digitization remains a vital and under-appreciated feature of the mining sector.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The investable edge is in data infrastructure and governance, not just rail - a gap that development partners and vendors could fill.
insights.quote: Digitization has increasingly become a vital and under-appreciated feature of the mining sector.
insights.locator: Digitization has increasingly become a vital

insights.claim: Online geological databases, traceability tools, transparency portals and mining cadastre systems (online land records of mining rights) are fundamental to regional mining ambitions but raise new governance challenges over what data is collected, who owns it, where it is stored and who can access it.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Data ownership and access rights become a new axis of value and contestation - watch which actors control the platforms.
insights.locator: Data systems – such as online geological databases

insights.claim: The Corridor's digital component has so far lagged behind physical infrastructure and was not the focus of the recent wave of investment announcements, even though it features in the long-term strategic vision.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A visible investment gap - inland connectivity and data systems are an entry point for development partners and a leading indicator of corridor credibility.
insights.quote: the Corridor’s digital component has so far lagged behind

insights.claim: The DRC recently signed a memorandum of understanding with Beijing for the construction of a 2,100-km fibre optic network along the Congo River, advancing China's digital footprint while the Western-backed Corridor's data layer lags.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: China is moving to control the region's digital backbone - a strategic counter to Western rail investment that shifts long-term data governance leverage.
insights.quote: the DRC recently signed a memorandum of understanding with Beijing for the construction of a 2100km fibre optic network along the Congo River
insights.locator: Meanwhile, the DRC recently signed a memorandum

insights.claim: Belgium has refused KoBold - a US company backed by Jeff Bezos and Bill Gates - access to a Belgian museum's colonial-era DRC geological archives, illustrating public-private tensions over who should digitize and own valuable geological records.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Access to legacy geological data is itself a contested asset - a bottleneck that could gate exploration advantages for well-capitalized entrants like KoBold.
insights.quote: the recent disagreement between the Belgian government and KoBold, a US company backed by Jeff Bezos and Bill Gates, over who should digitize the colonial-era geological records on the DRC
insights.locator: The digitized geological records of the DRC

insights.claim: Angola, the DRC and Zambia are implementing online cadastre systems meant to reduce overlapping claims, simplify allocation of rights and curb corruption, but these systems remain corruptible - sometimes more easily than hard copy.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Digitization is not a reliability guarantee - investors relying on official rights records should discount for manipulation risk.
insights.locator: Angola, the DRC and Zambia are implementing

insights.claim: Some mining companies have lost confidence in the region's digitized systems due to concession ownership allegedly being changed overnight, opaque back-end controls and limited audit trails.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Title uncertainty is a concrete deterrent to mining investment in the corridor - a due-diligence red flag until audit trails improve.
insights.quote: changes allegedly being made overnight to concession ownership, opaque back end controls and limited audit trails
insights.locator: This has led some mining companies to lose confidence

insights.claim: At DRC Mining Week in Lubumbashi in June, Chatham House heard that digital cadastres had often been operated by staff with limited experience working with computers, meaning digitization without capacity and safeguards risks re-encoding existing power imbalances.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Human-capacity gaps mean the promised transparency benefits may not materialize soon - a temper on optimism about governance reform.
insights.quote: digital cadastres had often been operated by staff with limited experience working with computers
insights.locator: Secondly, these systems often depend on human capacity

insights.claim: The DRC's state-owned certification agency CEEC has launched an E-trace digital platform to track and certify minerals across the value chain to ensure responsible sourcing, driven by the link between conflict and minerals in the east of the country.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Certification platforms are becoming table stakes for market access - compliance capability may differentiate suppliers to Western buyers.
insights.quote: The DRC’s state-owned certification agency (CEEC) has launched an E-trace digital platform to track and certify minerals across the value chain

insights.claim: There is a gap between what international markets want and what the DRC delivers: consumers seek mine-to-manufacturer provenance tracing, while the DRC treats traceability mainly as verifying that reported export volumes and qualities match what is actually taken out of the country.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Responsible-sourcing claims tied to DRC minerals may fall short of buyer expectations - a compliance and reputational risk for downstream manufacturers.
insights.quote: it stops short of a fuller, mine to manufacturer conception of traceability that would follow material all the way through the supply chain
insights.locator: The DRC’s emphasis reflects genuine concerns

insights.claim: Whether the Lobito Corridor becomes a coherent, credible space for investment and accountable development will depend heavily on how data systems interoperate, what they measure and who controls them, requiring interoperable standards and agreed protocols for data ownership and access.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Data interoperability is the decisive variable for corridor-wide investment credibility - a governance milestone to watch before scaling exposure.
insights.quote: How these systems talk to each other, what they measure, and who controls them will heavily influence whether the Lobito Corridor becomes a coherent, credible space for investment
insights.locator: How these systems talk to each other
